raw_consumer_statement:
I am disputing a charge of {$3400.00} on my American Express card ( Case : XXXX, XXXX : XXXX ). The merchant ( XXXX & XXXX XXXX ) claimed the package was delivered and signed for, but the delivery proof is fraudulent. 
The XXXX tracking shows the package was signed by XXXX XXXX. 
I live alone at the delivery address ( a single-family residence, confirmed by county property records ). No person named XXXX XXXX resides with me or was authorized to accept my deliveries. 
American Express accepted the merchants statement that this was my roommate, which is false. I provided proof that I have no roommate and that the property is a single-family residence. 
I also provided photos showing the box I did receive was damaged and not signed for by me. 
Despite this, Amex has sided with the merchant without properly reviewing the evidence, effectively ignoring that the delivery signature was not mine and not an authorized resident. 
This is a violation of the Fair Credit Billing Act ( Regulation Z ), which requires the card issuer to investigate billing errors ( including goods not received as agreed ). Amex closed my dispute while the merchant provided false and insufficient documentation.

I am requesting that the CFPB require American Express to : Properly review the dispute evidence. 
Reverse the chargeback denial, since the package was not signed for or received by me. 
Ensure Amex follows Regulation Z protections for cardholders.
